summary: Jeff Atwood humorously explores the widespread misuse of Comic Sans MS, a font he and his wife have turned into a running joke after spotting it in inappropriate contexts. He references the Ban Comic Sans movement, which argues that typefaces carry inherent meaning and using Comic Sans for serious purposes is like wearing a clown costume to a formal event. The post includes Vincent Connare's defense that he designed the font specifically for Microsoft Bob's cartoon dog character, never intending it for broader use. Atwood concludes by blaming Microsoft Bob for unleashing this typographic plague and jokes about the inevitable arrival of 'Comic Serif.'
key_points: Typefaces convey meaning independent of the words they display, making font choice a critical design decision
Comic Sans was designed for a narrow purpose (children's software with cartoon characters) but was inappropriately adopted for widespread use
The misuse of Comic Sans has become so prevalent that it spawned an organized movement to ban it
Microsoft Bob continues to be a source of regrettable software artifacts
money_quotes: Like the tone of a spoken voice, the characteristics of a typeface convey meaning. The design of the typeface is, in itself, its voice.
It is analogous to showing up for a black tie event in a clown costume.
There was no intention to include the font in other applications other than those designed for children when I designed Comic Sans.
The inspiration came at the shock of seeing Times New Roman used in an inappropriate way.
Proving, yet again, that anything which came from Microsoft Bob cannot be good.
At this point, we're bracing for the inevitable Comic Serif.
key_insight: Fonts carry inherent communicative weight beyond their literal text, and using them inappropriately undermines your message—a lesson the world has aggressively failed to learn with Comic Sans.
